coredata.dc:title: Efficient capacitive desalination over NCQDs decorated FeOOH composite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Peking University. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Yihan
Han, Xue
Zhang, Jie
Wen, Xiaoru
coredata.dc:description: Capacitive deionization (CDI) is emerging as a novel technology for seawater purification, with the electrode material playing a crucial role in desalination performance. In this study, we designed a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated iron oxide hydroxide (NCQDs/FeOOH) composite by a facile hydrothermal strategy and investigated as the CDI cathode for desalination application. Microstructural analyses reveal that the composite features a relatively uniform nanoparticle-assembled network, hierarchical pore alignment, and abundant porosity. Electrochemical tests confirm its outstanding capacitance property and conductivity. In an initial NaCl aqueous solution of 2000 ​mg ​L−1 ​at an applied potential of 1.4 ​V, the GACNaCl of NCQDs/FeOOH hybrid electrode reaches 56.52 ​mg ​g−1, along with the remarkable cycling durability. Furthermore, CV (cyclic voltammetry) and ex situ XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) characterizations indicate the predominantly pseudocapacitive desalination mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
FeOOH
Composite material
CDI desalination
Durability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic illustrations of the fabrication process for NCQDs/FeOOH; The high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Fe 2p, (c) O 1s, (d) C 1s, and (e) N 1s for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH.

(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) Raman spectra, (c) XRD patterns, (h) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, and (i) pore size distribution curves for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH. The (d) SEM, (e–f) TEM, and (g) element mapping images for NCQDs/FeOOH.

(a) CV plots at a scan rate of 5 ​mV ​s−1, (c) C m vs. scan rates plots, (d) Nyquist plots, (e) the real part impedance (Z′) vs. squared root of the angular frequency (ω −1/2), (f) GCD plots at a current load of 400 ​mA ​g−1, and (g) iR drops vs. current loads plots for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH; (b) CV plots at scan rates varying from 1 to 100 ​mV ​s−1 and (h) the Coulomb efficiency/capacity retention vs. cycle plots for NCQDs/FeOOH (the inset is the cycling curve at 1500 ​mA ​g−1).

(a) The GACNaCl vs. working time and (b) Ragone plots for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH; The GACNaCl vs. working time plots for the NCQDs/FeOOH upon different applied voltages (c) and in different NaCl aqueous solution concentration (d); (e) The Coulomb efficiency/capacity retention vs. cycle plots for NCQDs/FeOOH//AC CDI cell (the inset is the cycling curve at 1200 ​mA ​g−1).

The high-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Na 1s and (b) Fe 2p before and after the CDI desalnation process, (c) Log(i) vs. Log(v) plots, CV curve at a scan rate of 5 ​mV ​s−1, and (e) schematic illustrations of CDI desalnation mechanism for NCQDs/FeOOH.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: The project was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region of China (2023MS02010) and the Program for Young Talents of Science and Technology in Universities of Inner Mongolia Autonomous Region (NJYT23032).

head.ce:title: Efficient capacitive desalination over NCQDs decorated FeOOH composite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010021, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Capacitive deionization (CDI) is emerging as a novel technology for seawater purification, with the electrode material playing a crucial role in desalination performance. In this study, we designed a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots decorated iron oxide hydroxide (NCQDs/FeOOH) composite by a facile hydrothermal strategy and investigated as the CDI cathode for desalination application. Microstructural analyses reveal that the composite features a relatively uniform nanoparticle-assembled network, hierarchical pore alignment, and abundant porosity. Electrochemical tests confirm its outstanding capacitance property and conductivity. In an initial NaCl aqueous solution of 2000 ​mg ​L−1 ​at an applied potential of 1.4 ​V, the GACNaCl of NCQDs/FeOOH hybrid electrode reaches 56.52 ​mg ​g−1, along with the remarkable cycling durability. Furthermore, CV (cyclic voltammetry) and ex situ XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) characterizations indicate the predominantly pseudocapacitive desalination mechanism.

The robust NCQDs/FeOOH hybrid CDI electrode was developed. The hybrid electrode proved a remarkable GACNaCl of 56.52 ​mg ​g−1 in a 2000 ​mg ​L−1 NaCl solution with excellent durability. The hybrid electrode revealed the predominantly pseudocapacitive desalination mechanism.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Global environmental deterioration and population surge have led to the freshwater resource crisis, making it one of the most significant energy crises facing the world this century [1]. Simultaneously, the freshwater scarcity is expected to worsen as a result of the climate change and acceleration of urbanization and industrialization. Therefore, the advancements in water purification technologies have quickly progressed, with the seawater/brackish water desalination considered a key solution to address this crisis [2,3].

Compared to traditional desalination technologies, capacitive deionization (CDI) stands out for its environmental friendliness, low energy consumption, low cost, high adsorption capacity, and ease of regeneration, making it a highly promising option for seawater desalination [4–6]. CDI is an advanced water treatment technology driven by a voltage, wherein salt ions are adsorbed onto the oppositely charged electrode surfaces based on the electrochemical double-layer or Faradaic reaction mechanisms [7,8]. Generally, under the applied voltage, anions and cations in the saline solution migrate towards the anode and cathode, respectively, and are subsequently adsorbed, leading to their synergetic removal. Following the adsorption saturation, the adsorbed ions can be desorbed effectively by applying a reverse voltage and returned back to the bulk solution, facilitating the effective regeneration of electrode [9,10]. Noteworthy, the electrode material plays a crucial role in CDI technology. High-performance electrode materials should possess key advantages such as low cost, high specific surface area, high conductivity, high theoretical capacity, and excellent chemical/physical stability. Currently, carbon-based electrode materials, including graphene, activated carbon, and carbon nanotubes, which are based on the electrochemical double-layer mechanism, are extensively utilized in the CDI field due to their abundant raw materials, tunable pore sizes, good conductivity, and high specific surface areas. Nevertheless, the significant co-ion exclusion effects, low capacity, and complicated preparation processing severely limit their practical CDI desalination application [11–14]. In regard to these major issues, the Faradaic CDI electrode materials based on the reversible redox reaction mechanism, featuring with the high adsorption capacity, excellent ion selectivity, and outstanding cycling stability, have gained worldwide attention in recent years. The currently widely studied pseudocapacitive electrode materials primarily comprise the NASICON, Prussian blue, transition metal compounds (TMCs), and their composites [15–18].

In recent years, TMCs such as sulfides, carbides, nitrides, oxides, hydroxides, and oxide hydroxides have gained widespread utilization in the CDI field for seawater/brackish water treatment due to their remarkable physical/chemical properties, including high theoretical specific capacity, excellent conductivity, and outstanding cycling stability [19–22]. Notably, the iron oxide hydroxide (FeOOH) is distinguished by its natural abundance, high capacity, high redox activity, and unique open structure. As a Faradaic electrode material, the FeOOH has garnered considerable attention in applications such as supercapacitors and sodium/lithium/zinc ion batteries, demonstrating its potential advantages in high-performance CDI desalination [23–27]. Shen et al. develop a three-dimensional “urchin-like” HCSs@FeOOH CDI anode by incorporating the hollow carbon spheres (HCSs) scaffold and FeOOH nanorods, achieving a Cl− adsorption capacity of 69.48 ​mg ​g−1 in a 3000 ​mg ​L−1 NaCl solution, with only a 14.32% decrease in capacity even after 100 cycles [28]. Our research group also proposes the ion doped FeOOH heterostructures (e.g., Na-FeOOH and Cl-FeOOH) by a one-step hydrothermal technique, serving as the cathode and anode, respectively, to construct a novel asymmetric CDI device (Na-FeOOH//Cl-FeOOH). This device demonstrates an attractive desalination capacity of 35.12 ​mg ​g−1 in a 500 ​mg ​L−1 NaCl solution at 1.2 ​V along with the exceptional durability [29]. Nevertheless, the FeOOH-based CDI electrodes continue to encounter significant limitations, including the restricted adsorption capacity, electrochemical conductivity, and cycling stability. Hence, it is of great significance to explore novel high-performance FeOOH-based electrodes via the morphology engineering, doping modification, and heterostructure design.

Surface modification of carbon materials is an effective way to enhance the electrochemical performance of TMCs. On one hand, carbon materials can enhance the electrochemical conductivity of TMCs. Meanwhile, the incorporated carbon provides more active adsorption sites for salt ions based on the double-layer mechanism, which is beneficial for the further increasing adsorption capacity. Additionally, the rigid carbon framework can alleviate the structural degradation of TMCs during the cycling process, further improving the stability [30–33]. Among the various carbon derivatives, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), specifically those doped with heteroatoms such as sulfur, phosphorus, boron, or nitrogen (N), have gained significant attention due to their excellent physical/chemical stability, high specific surface area, good hydrophilicity, and exceptional conductivity in the fields of photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, supercapacitors, and batteries [34–37]. Recent studies have demonstrated that, apart from the optimized electronic structure, enhanced crystal stability, and increased active adsorption sites, the CQDs modification can also induce the surface morphology alteration of TMCs, resulting in the improved ion diffusion/adsorption, ultimately boosting the electrochemical performance [38,39].

In this work, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) modified iron oxide hydroxide (FeOOH) composite (NCQDs/FeOOH) was constructed by a facile one-step hydrothermal avenue (Fig. 1 a), and an asymmetric CDI device was assembled by the NCQDs/FeOOH cathode and activated carbon (AC) anode for desalination application. The effects of NCQDs incorporation on the microstructure features (e.g., surface morphology, crystal structure, and crystallinity), electrochemical properties, and CDI desalination performance including capacity, desalination rate, and cyclic durability were thoroughly examined. Additionally, ex situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis were carried out to reveal the ion capture mechanism, and the predominantly pseudocapacitive desalination mechanism was revealed for NCQDs/FeOOH hybrid.

The NCQDs/FeOOH composite materials were fabricated by a hydrothermal avenue. Initially, the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) suspension was obtained with the urea (NH2CONH2, ≥ 99.0%) and citric acid (C6H8O·H2O, ≥ 99.5%) precursors in a hydrothermal process [40]. Subsequently, 334 ​mg of anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3, 98.0%) and 12 ​mg of urea were completely dissolved in 17 ​mL of deionized H2O, after which 3 ​mL of the as-prepared NCQD suspension was added. After ultrasonication for 20 ​min at room temperature, the mixture was transferred to a 50 ​mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 100 ​°C for 12 ​h. Afterwards, the product was cooled to room temperature and then sequentially rinsed with H2O and ethanol during the filtration process until the solution pH approaching 7, where was further vacuum-dried at 60 ​°C for 12 ​h. For comparison, NCQDs/FeOOH composite materials with varying NCQD contents and pristine FeOOH were synthesized using a similar hydrothermal method (S1.1, Supporting Information).

The phase and structural analysis were conducted using X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS-D8) with a Cu-K α target along with an incident wavelength of 1.5406 ​Å, scanning range of 5°–60°, a tube current of 40 ​mA, and voltage of 40 ​kV. The surface elemental composition and valence states were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, XSAM800), with an excitation source of Al-K α radiation (1486.67 ​eV), a power of 200 ​W, and a vacuum level of 10−9 torr. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker VERTEX70) was employed to analyze the chemical composition. The surface morphology and structure were revealed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200S). Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR Evolution) was recorded to further analyze the structure and chemical composition. The specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume were assessed through nitrogen adsorption/desorption measurements (BELSORP-mini II). The specific surface area and pore size distribution were calculated according to the BET method and the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) model, respectively.

Film electrodes were prepared by a slurry coating processing, wherein the active materials (NCQDs/FeOOH or FeOOH), carbon black, and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) were mixed uniformly with a weight ratio of 8 : 1: 1. The mixture was uniformly coated onto the conductive graphite paper, dried at 120 ​°C for 12 ​h, and resulted in a film with 10 ​mg of active material. Electrochemical performance tests including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) measurements, were conducted in a three-electrode system with the as-prepared electrode, saturated calomel electrode, and graphite paper acting as working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively, in a 1 ​mol ​L−1 NaCl solution at room temperature. The detailed characterizations and calculation methods were further provided in Supporting Information (S1.2).

The CDI electrode processing is similar to that of electrochemical performance analysis with the active material mass of 0.08 ​g. Asymmetric CDI devices (NCQDs/FeOOH//AC) were assembled with the NCQDs/FeOOH cathode and AC anode to evaluate the desalination performance in a simulated NaCl solution. The running parameters including the voltage, solution volume (V), solution concentration (C 0, mg L−1), and flow rate were 1.0–1.4 ​V, 55 ​mL, 800–2000 ​mg ​L−1, and 40 ​mL ​min−1, respectively. The outlet solution conductivity was dynamically monitored to calculate the ion concentration of cell outlet (C, mg L−1)). The gravimetric adsorption capacity (GACNaCl, mg g−1) and gravimetric adsorption rate (GAR, mg (g·min)−1) are as follows:
(1)
GAC NaCl
= 0.5
C 0
-
C
V / m
(2)
GAR =
GAC NaCl
/ t
where the t (min) represents the operating time of CDI cell.

The surface elemental composition and valence states were revealed by the XPS technique. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information), the presence of elements such as Fe, O, and C is recorded in the full-range XPS spectra for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH. In the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum of pristine FeOOH (Fig. 1b), the doublets at 713.3/727.0 ​eV and 710.4/724.3 ​eV correspond to Fe 2p 3/2/Fe 2p 1/2 of Fe3+ and Fe2+, respectively [23,41]. Additionally, the peaks focused at 529.5, 531.8, and 533.3 ​eV in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) can be attributed to Fe−O, Fe−OH, and H−OH bonds in sequence [29,42]. As illustrated, the NCQDs/FeOOH composite exhibits the similar Fe 2p and O 1s XPS spectra to those of pristine FeOOH, indicating the relatively stable structure even after the NCQDs incorporation. Notably, in comparison to those of the FeOOH, the characteristic peaks for Fe3+ (714.0/727.5 ​eV) and Fe2+ (711.0/724.7 ​eV) of the NCQDs/FeOOH composite exhibit a relative shift, which is primarily ascribed to the intense charge transfer interactions between the FeOOH and NCQDs [43,44]. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d), the peaks focused at 284.8, 286.6, and 288.5 ​eV correspond to the C−C/C=C, C−O/C−N, and C=O/C=N [45,46], respectively, while the presence of carbon peaks in the pristine FeOOH may result from the inevitably surface contamination. Furthermore, in the N 1s spectrum of NCQDs/FeOOH, the peaks at 399.7, 400.6, and 398.1 ​eV (Fig. 1e), belonging to the pyrrole-N, graphite-N, and pyridine-N [11,47], are significantly enhanced compared to the FeOOH, which mainly results from the NCQDs incorporation. Generally, the pyrrole-N contributes to promote the surface wettability of electrode in the electrolyte solution, thereby facilitating the Faradaic reactions, while the graphite-N benefits the enhanced electronic conductivity [48,49]. Therefore, the XPS results indicate that the NCQDs-modified FeOOH-based heterostructure has been successfully constructed via the facile hydrothermal method. Additionally, the contact angle analyses are shown in Fig. S2. Clearly, compared to the pristine FeOOH electrode, the surface water contact angle of NCQDs/FeOOH with the NCQDs incorporation is evidently reduced, suggesting its better wettability property.

The surface functional groups for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH were monitored by FT-IR, as illustrated in Fig. 2 a. A peak at approximately 3500 ​cm−1 can be indexed to the characteristic vibrations of −OH [50]. The peaks observed at 817, 697, and 637 ​cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of Fe−O(H)−Fe, FeO6 coordination octahedron unit cell, and Fe−O stretching vibrations, respectively [51,52], evidencing the successful construction of β-FeOOH phase via the facile hydrothermal process. Noteworthy, compared to that of pristine FeOOH, the FT-IR spectrum of NCQDs/FeOOH composite displays several intense peaks at 1787, 1603, 1392, 1303, and 1165 ​cm−1, which are ascribed to the characteristic absorption peaks of C=O, C=C, −NH, C−N, and C−O [53,54]. The Raman spectra of NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH are depicted in Fig. 2b, with peaks at 216, 279, 394, 488, 592, and 1297 ​cm−1 corresponding to the vibrational modes of FeOOH [44,55]. In comparison to FeOOH, the composite material exhibits a strong new peak at 1577 ​cm−1, belonging to the G band of NCQDs, indicating the high graphitization degree, which facilitates the enhanced conductivity [56]. Additionally, the intensity of broad peak at around 1300 ​cm−1 increases relatively, mainly due to the presence of D band from NCQDs guest in the NCQDs/FeOOH composite [57], further confirming the NCQDs incorporation towards the FeOOH framework.

The crystal structure and phase composition of both FeOOH and NCQDs/FeOOH were investigated by XRD. As shown in Fig. 2c, the FeOOH exhibits distinct peaks at approximately 11.9°, 16.8°, 26.8°, 34.1°, 35.2°, 39.3°, 46.5°, 52.1°, and 56.0°, corresponding to the (110), (200), (310), (400), (211), (301), (411), (600), and (521) planes of β-FeOOH phase (JCPDS No. 34-1266) [58–60]. The observations indicate that the well-crystallized FeOOH structure with a polycrystalline feature has been produced through the hydrothermal reaction. Notably, significant changes are observed in the XRD pattern of NCQDs/FeOOH with the NCQDs incorporation. In Detail, the aforementioned main characteristic peaks of FeOOH disappear, suggesting its amorphous structure, potentially improving the structural stability and electrochemical performance [61]. Additionally, the absence of peak at around 24° is deemed to be associated with the relatively low content of NCQDs loading [62]. Furthermore, the weak peak detected at about 20° for NCQDs/FeOOH can be assigned to the SiO2 substrate [63].

SEM measurement was utilized to observe the apparent morphology and microstructure for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH. As illustrated in Fig. S3a, the pristine FeOOH showcases a relatively uniform and randomly dispersed nanorod structure with pores or cracks. Post modification with the NCQDs, the surface morphology of NCQDs/FeOOH displays evident changes, featuring aggregated structures composed of numerous small and uniformly distributed nanoparticles with abundant porosity, which can boost the active surface area and fast ion diffusion/transfer (Fig. 2d). Consequently, the surface modification of NCQDs facilitates the morphology transformation of composite [38,39]. The TEM images of NCQDs/FeOOH in Fig. 2e and f and insets reveal a uniform nanoparticle morphology with numerous nanopores. Meanwhile, the absence of distinct lattice fringes further indicates its amorphous structure. Additionally, the lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 ​nm can be attributed to the (100) crystal plane of NCQDs. Elemental distribution maps (Fig. 2g and Fig. S3b) and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra (Fig. S3c) confirm the uniform distribution of Fe, O, C, and N elements, with the relative atomic ratios of approximately 10.50%, 32.54%, 48.93%, and 8.03%, respectively. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH are shown in Fig. 2h. The NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH exhibit similar isotherms, with a hysteresis loop in the medium pressure region and substantial adsorption behavior at a relative pressure reaching 1.0, verifying the mesoporous and macroporous properties [64,65]. Based on calculations, the specific surface area (S BET) and BJH pore volume (V BJH) of NCQDs/FeOOH (137.18 ​m2 ​g−1 and 0.47 ​cm3 ​g−1) are relatively increased compared to those of FeOOH (33.07 ​m2 ​g−1 and 0.25 ​cm3 ​g−1). In addition, the pore size distribution curve indicates that the composite has an average pore size of approximately 11.87 ​nm, confirming its mesoporous characteristic. The hierarchical porous alignment and abundant porosity of NCQDs/FeOOH enables more exposed active sites and accelerated ion diffusion/transfer property, further boosting CDI adsorption capacity and cycling stability [38].

The capacitive performance, conductivity, and cycling durability of electrode materials are key factors for practical CDI desalination. The capacitive property was evaluated through CV tests in a 1 ​mol ​L−1 NaCl solution over a voltage range of −0.9 to 0.2 ​V. The resultant CV curves of NCQDs/FeOOH with different NCQDs contents are displayed in Fig. S4. As illustrated, the curves exhibit quasi-rectangular shapes, indicating the excellent capacitive features. With the increasing NCQDs loading, the specific capacitance (C m) initially increases and then decreases to some extent, reaching the maximum when 3 ​mL of the NCQDs suspension is added in the precursor solution. Moreover, the CV curves at a scan rate of 5 ​mV ​s−1 for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH are presented in Fig. 3 a. The composite electrode obtains an enlarged enclosed CV area compared to pristine FeOOH, suggesting its enhanced capacitive performance. The calculated C m of NCQDs/FeOOH reaches 157 ​F ​g−1, which is notably higher than that of FeOOH (122 ​F ​g−1), indicating its superior ion capture capability. The CV curves at various rates (1–100 ​mV ​s−1) are illustrated in Fig. 3b and S5. As the rate increases, the current gradually increases, causing the quasi-rectangular CV shapes to deform. It ultimately leads to a gradual C m decrease, which is primarily attributed to the reduced utilization of active surface and increased internal resistance to salt ion diffusion under the high rate [38,66]. Notably, the NCQDs/FeOOH electrode exhibits superior capacitive performance at all rates, as shown in Fig. 3c.

The electrochemical conductivity of electrodes was assessed by EIS technique. As seen in Fig. 3d, the Nyquist plots of NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH electrodes show similar patterns, with a quasi-semicircular shape at high-frequency pattern and a linear section at low-frequency region. Notably, the plot for NCQDs/FeOOH shows a smaller intercept value on the horizontal axis compared to pristine FeOOH (the inset of Fig. 3d), indicating a lower equivalent series resistance. The optimized equivalent circuit model is also illustrated, where the R s, R ct, W, and CPE represent the solution resistance, charge transfer resistance, Warburg resistance, and constant phase element, respectively. The R ct and R s values for pristine FeOOH are calculated to be 2.97 and 2.89 ​Ω, while those for NCQDs/FeOOH decrease to 2.36 and 1.64 ​Ω, respectively, further proving that NCQDs incorporation can improve the electronic conductivity of FeOOH network. Additionally, the diffusion coefficient (
D
Na +
) for NCQDs/FeOOH is determined to be 2.11 ​× ​10−15 ​cm2 ​s−1, which shows much superiority than that of FeOOH (1.86 ​× ​10−15 ​cm2 ​s−1) (Fig. 3e), indicating the enhanced ion diffusion/transport property of composite electrode.

The capacitive feature, conductivity, and cycling stability were further assessed by the GCD tests. The resultant curves of NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH at various current loads (200–1000 ​mA ​g−1) exhibit the quasi-triangular patterns (Fig. S6), suggesting the appealing capacitive property. The discharge time of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode at a current density of 400 ​mA ​g−1 is notably longer than that of pristine FeOOH, corresponding to the larger capacitance (Fig. 3f), aligning with CV observations. The iR drop vs. current density plots show that the iR drop rises along with the enlarged current density, while the composite electrode demonstrates a reduced value (Fig. 3g), confirming its enhanced conductivity capability, agreeing well with EIS results. Furthermore, the GCD cycling curve of composite electrode at a current density of 1500 ​mA ​g−1 is illustrated in Fig. 3h. As observed, the recorded curve shape remains consistent even after 12000 cycles along with a coulombic efficiency of about 100% and a capacity retention of up to 92%. It showcases the exceptional cyclic durability of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode, primarily due to the synergistic effect of the unique microstructure and highly conductive NCQDs incorporation. In brief, the coupling of CV, EIS, and GCD tests verify that the as-constructed composite electrode deliver superior capacitive performance and electrochemical conductivity, and remarkable durability, showing potential advantages for efficient CDI desalination.

Assembled with either the NCQDs/FeOOH or FeOOH cathode and AC anode, the asymmetric CDI devices (NCQDs/FeOOH//AC or FeOOH//AC) were examined in simulated saline solutions to investigate their potential deionization capabilities (Fig. S7). The Na ions in the solution diffuse and intercalate towards the NCQDs/FeOOH electrode upon the external voltage, while Cl ions adsorb onto the AC electrode, thus achieving effective CDI desalination. The outlet solution conductivity vs. time curves for the NCQDs/FeOOH//AC and FeOOH//AC devices under an applied voltage of 1.4 ​V in a NaCl solution concentration of 2000 ​mg ​L−1 are illustrated in Fig. S8. The solution conductivity notably decreases with the prolonged operation time, eventually stabilizing after 120 ​min, indicating the synergistic removal of Na+ and Cl−. The NCQDs/FeOOH device exhibits a reduced solution conductivity compared to the pristine cell, showcasing its superior ion capture capability. The GACNaCl for NCQDs/FeOOH electrode is calculated to be 56.52 ​mg ​g−1, showing a 61% increase compared to FeOOH (35.18 ​mg ​g−1) (Fig. 4 a), directly confirming its superior desalination property. The Ragone plots for NCQDs/FeOOH and FeOOH electrodes are displayed in Fig. 4b. The plot in the upper right region for the NCQDs/FeOOH indicates the higher capacity and faster rate compared to pristine FeOOH. Additionally, the impact of various voltages (1.0–1.4 ​V) and solution concentrations (800–2000 ​mg ​L−1) on the deionization efficiency of composite electrode are also examined. Under higher applied voltages, the NCQDs/FeOOH electrode-based cell exhibits lower outlet solution conductivity and higher desalination capacity (Fig. 4c and S9). The GACNaCl increases from 33.56 to 56.52 ​mg ​g−1 along with the expanded voltage from 1.0 to 1.4 ​V, primarily due to the significantly enhanced electrostatic attraction at the electrode/solution interface. Moreover, as the salt solution concentration increases from 800 to 2000 ​mg ​L−1, the GACNaCl increases from 27.96 to 56.52 ​mg ​g−1. Thus, the applied voltage and solution concentration significantly affect the CDI desalination performance of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode, with relatively higher voltages and concentrations favoring the enhanced ion capture capability. Furthermore, to investigate the cyclic durability of NCQDs/FeOOH//AC CDI cell, the GCD cycling tests were also conducted. The resultant curve at a current density of 1200 ​mA ​g−1 is shown in Fig. 4e. As illustrated, after approximately 20000 cycles, the curve still maintains quasi-triangular shape well, with both the coulombic efficiency and capacity retention rate reaching 100%, further proving the attractive cycling stability of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode.

Furthermore, the CDI desalination mechanism of NCQDs/FeOOH was further investigated through ex situ XPS and CV techniques, as shown in Fig. 5 and S11–S13. As illustrated, the XPS spectra after the CDI adsorption process exhibits a pronounced Na 1s peak (Fig. 5a), indicating the effective Na+ capture onto the NCQDs/FeOOH cathode under the driven voltage. In the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum of Na ​+ ​-saturated NCQDs/FeOOH electrode, a decreased Fe3+ content along with an increased Fe2+ one, in comparison to those of the fresh electrode (Fig. 5b), suggests the primarily Faradaic reaction of Fe3+/Fe2+ couple induced by the efficient Na ​+ ​intercalation during the CDI process. Additionally, Fig. S13 displays the CV curves in a 1 ​mol ​L−1 NaCl solution at scan rates varying from 0.2 to 5 ​mV ​s−1. The presence of redox peaks indicates the reversible Faradaic reaction driven by the salt ion intercalation/deintercalation towards the NCQDs/FeOOH electrode network. The Faradaic capacitance contribution of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode gradually rises with the increasing rate, reaching 62% at 5 ​mV ​s−1 (Fig. 5c and d), further verifying the main pseudocapacitive ion capture mechanism (Fig. 5e).

In summary, this work reported a hydrothermal method to construct the NCQDs/FeOOH heterostructure utilized as the CDI cathode, and investigated its desalination characteristics. The surface morphology, crystal structure, and crystallinity were characterized by XPS, FT-IR, Raman, XRD, and SEM. Benefiting from the synergistic effects of unique microstructure, outstanding conductivity and stability of NCQDs incorporation, and high theoretical capacity of FeOOH, the NCQDs/FeOOH electrode exhibits the improved capacitance and electronic conductivity. Meanwhile, CDI performance tests indicate that the GACNaCl of composite electrode reaches 56.52 ​mg ​g−1 in a NaCl concentration of 2000 ​mg ​L−1 upon an applied voltage of 1.4 ​V, along with the remarkable cyclic durability, which is superior to that of pristine electrode (35.18 ​mg ​g−1). Additionally, CV and ex situ XPS tests reveal that desalination mechanism of NCQDs/FeOOH electrode is primarily dominated by Faradaic reactions.

Yihan Xue: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Xue Han: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Jie Zhang: Software, Data curation. Xiaoru Wen: Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
